coredata.dc:title: Ultra-sensitive detection of Cu2+ in water and infant formula using a carbon dot-embedded MIL-53 electrochemiluminescence sensor

coredata.dc:description: This study synthesises a novel N-CQDs@MIL-53 composite via a hydro-thermal method for fabricating an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor to detect Cu2+ ions. The sensor delivers remarkable performance in detecting Cu2+ ions, achieving a broad detection range from 5 × 10−8 to 1 × 10−4 mol/L and a remarkable limit of detection of 1.44 × 10−8 mol/L. This innovative approach exhibits high efficiency in analysing Cu2+ both in infant formula and water samples, yielding results comparable to those obtained using the standard ICP method. Notably, the N-CQDs@MIL-53 composite based ECL sensor outperforms its counterpart fabricated using standalone N-CQDs, offering enhanced sensitivity and a wider linear detection range. Moreover, the integration of N-CQDs into MIL-53 considerably enhances the composite's storage stability. This approach provides a simple, and cost-effective strategy for Cu2+ analysis in actual samples; in addition, it introduces a key concept of enhancing the performance of CQD-based ECL sensors by incorporating them into metal organic frameworks (MOFs).

head.ce:title: Ultra-sensitive detection of Cu2+ in water and infant formula using a carbon dot-embedded MIL-53 electrochemiluminescence sensor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study synthesises a novel N-CQDs@MIL-53 composite via a hydro-thermal method for fabricating an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor to detect Cu2+ ions. The sensor delivers remarkable performance in detecting Cu2+ ions, achieving a broad detection range from 5 × 10−8 to 1 × 10−4 mol/L and a remarkable limit of detection of 1.44 × 10−8 mol/L. This innovative approach exhibits high efficiency in analysing Cu2+ both in infant formula and water samples, yielding results comparable to those obtained using the standard ICP method. Notably, the N-CQDs@MIL-53 composite based ECL sensor outperforms its counterpart fabricated using standalone N-CQDs, offering enhanced sensitivity and a wider linear detection range. Moreover, the integration of N-CQDs into MIL-53 considerably enhances the composite's storage stability. This approach provides a simple, and cost-effective strategy for Cu2+ analysis in actual samples; in addition, it introduces a key concept of enhancing the performance of CQD-based ECL sensors by incorporating them into metal organic frameworks (MOFs).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper (II) is the third most abundant trace element in living organisms and eco-systems, following iron (III) and zinc (II)(Chandrasekhar et al., 2012). It plays a crucial role in various proteins and enzymes that are essential for maintaining normal brain function, and it is involved in metabolism, and oxidative stress prevention(D'Ambrosi & Rossi, 2015; S. Zhang et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2023). Copper ions (Cu2+) are widely present in aquatic environments, where they remain stable and bioaccumulate in organisms through ecological cycles(Parsaee, Haratipour, Lariche, & Vojood, 2018). In addition, Cu2+ is extensively employed as a key component in plant protection products and as an additive in industrial and culinary applications(Qiu et al., 2020). Although organisms possess regulatory mechanisms to maintain a delicate balance between the essential role of Cu2+ and its potential toxicity at both the organ and cellular levels(Taki, Iyoshi, Ojida, Hamachi, & Yamamoto, 2010), disruptions in intra-cellular Cu2+ homeostasis can lead to protein aggregation or misfolding, which may contribute to severe neuro-degenerative diseases(Jo et al., 2012). Excess Cu2+ may also affect behaviour and embryonic development in organisms(Carotenuto et al., 2020). Consequently, precise and highly selective detection of Cu2+ is critical to ensuring food and environmental safety.

To date, Cu2+ detection has been carried out using a variety of analytical methods, including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS), atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), and titration(Avramescu et al., 2024; Michael et al., 2022; Nawaz et al., 2017; X. J. Wang et al., 2024). GC–MS requires trained professionals to operate the instrument, and its sample pretreatment procedures are often cumbersome, with high instrument costs(Lan, Yao, Ping, & Ying, 2017). Although AAS and ICP-MS exhibit high selectivity and sensitivity, and are widely used for detecting chemical contaminants in food due to their accuracy and precision, these methods also rely on sophisticated instrumentation and are time-consuming. Particularly for the detection of metal ions in water samples, complex chemical extraction processes are typically required, which may alter the speciation of metal ions, thereby affecting the accuracy of the detection results(Zhou et al., 2016). Hence, there is an urgent need to develop detection methods that integrate high sensitivity, remarkable selectivity, rapid analysis, and ease of use.

In recent years, a variety of innovative techniques have emerged for the detection of heavy metal ions, including membrane separation, adsorption, fluorescence (FL), and electro-chemical methods. However, membrane separation and adsorption methods often exhibit lower sensitivity, with membrane separation relying on a complex layer-by-layer electrostatic assembly process(Deng, Zhao, Meng, Zhang, & Hu, 2014; Li, Xi, Wang, & Cheng, 2023). Of these techniques, the FL-based method has gained widespread application in Cu2+ detection, owing to its simplicity, enhanced selectivity, and ability to perform real-time monitoring. Nevertheless, the quenching effect of Cu2+ in most ‘turn-off’ fluorescent sensors can result in false-positive readings(Shen et al., 2020), while ‘turn-on’ fluorescent sensor probes often suffer poor solubility in aqueous environments(Tachapermpon et al., 2017). Meanwhile, electro-chemical methods, particularly electrochemiluminescence (ECL) methods, have garnered considerable attention from researchers because of their promising potential.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) integrates the essential features of both electro-chemical and chemi-luminescent processes. In ECL, the transfer of high-energy electrons between electro-active species generates an excited state, resulting in luminescence upon applying an electrical potential is applied(Cao, Shu, Qin, Su, & Peng, 2020; Wu, Cai, Zhang, Guo, & Jie, 2025; Xia, Hua, & Long, 2019). This technique offers numerous advantages, including affordability, ease of operation, ultra-high sensitivity, a broad detection range, and low background from solution diffusion effects(Mortazavi, Hosseini, Xu, Naderi-Manesh, & Ganjali, 2024; L. Q. Xie, Huang, & Su, 2017). Thus, ECL has attracted extensive interest across various domains including immunoassays, sensing, and imaging(Lai et al., 2024; W. S. Liu, Liu, Xiao, Jin, & Wu, 2024). Furthermore, numerous novel ECL emitters have been developed, including quantum dots (QDs), metal nanoclusters, perovskites, and carbon-based nanomaterials(X. Zhang, Wang, Nie, & Ma, 2021). Notably, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, have attracted considerable interest because of their tunable optical characteristics, minimal toxicity and remarkable bio-compatibility(Adsetts, Hoesterey, Gao, Love, & Ding, 2020; Niu et al., 2018). Therefore, they are well-suited to serve as luminescent centres in ECL sensors(Xiao et al., 2024), which have been effectively utilised for the detection of proteins, metal ions, cells and DNA/RNA. However, CQDs suffer challenges related to sensitivity and stability because of their inherently weak ECL signals, limited pH stability, and susceptibility to aggregation and quenching at high concentrations(Wang et al., 2024; Y. Q. Yang et al., 2024). To achieve higher effectiveness in ECL applications, carbon dots still require further modification or treatment.

To enhance the functional performance of CQDs, their surface architecture can be enhanced via the incorporation of dopant elements or via combination with graphene oxide (GO)/gold (Au) nanocomposites and zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8)/Au composites(Dong et al., 2012; Liang et al., 2021). Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are extensively utilised as luminous carriers for enhancing ECL signals because of their extensive surface area and tuneable pore sizes(Hussain et al., 2024). In particular, MIL(Fe), fabricated using Fe3+ and p-Phthalic acid (H2BDC), features high specific surface area and remarkable stability in aqueous solutions, attributed to the coordination bonds between Fe(III) and carboxylic acid groups(Bu et al., 2023). Moreover, the redox peak potentials of MIL(Fe) align with those of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−,resulting in a synergistic enhancement of the peak current that enables MIL(Fe) to serve as an efficient electronic medium, facilitating the charge transfer process(An et al., 2019). While ZIF-8/CQDs composites have been effectively used in ECL sensing for monitoring apoptosis and detecting miRNA, there lies a notable gap in the literature regarding the application of MIL(Fe)/CQDs-derived composites in ECL sensing.

Herein, we synthesise an N-CQDs@MIL-53 composite by embedding nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) into the MIL-53 framework via a hydro-thermal method. Leveraging the remarkable ECL properties of the N-CQDs@MIL-53 composite and the quenching effect of Cu2+ on its ECL intensity, we develop a highly sensitive ECL sensor for the quantification of Cu2+, as shown in Scheme 1 . We also evaluate and compare the Cu2+ detection performances of an ECL sensor based on N-CQDs and that based on N-CQDs@MIL-53. The results clearly indicate that the N-CQDs@MIL-53-based ECL sensor offers significant improvements both in detection limits and detection range, making it ideal for sensitive and selective Cu2+ detection in water and infant formula samples. This study highlights the promising potential of this sensor for rapid Cu2+ detection in the domain of food and water quality monitoring.

Chitosan and cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O) were purchased from Macklin, ammonium citrate, ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]) and potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]·3H2O) from Beijing Chemical Plant, p-Phthalic acid (H2BDC) from Aladdin, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Company, Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), copper sulfate (CuSO4·5H2O) were purchased from Sinopod Chemical Reagent Co., LTD., Sodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), cadmium chloride (CdCl2) and calcium chloride hexahydrate (CaCl2·6H2O) were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Science and Technology Development Co., LTD., potassium chloride (KCl) was purchased from Tianjin Beihua Glass Purchasing Center, barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2·2H2O) was purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., LTD., magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2) was purchased from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co., LTD., nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O) was purchased from Beijing Red Star Chemical Plant. The phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 mol/L, pH = 6) is a proportionally mixed solution of sodium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, and potassium chloride (0.1 mol/L). The remaining chemical reagents are analytical pure reagents and can be used without further purification.

A glassy carbon electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a platinum wire electrode were purchased from Tianjin Aida Hengsheng Technology Development Co., Ltd. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were captured using a Hitachi SU8000 microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded with a PANalytic B.V. instrument, while X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a 756PC UV–vis spectrophotometer, and fluorescence (FL) spectra were acquired using an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected with a Nicolet iS50 spectrophotometer. Electrochemical measurements were conducted on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, China), while ECL was measured using the CHI 660E electrochemical workstation in conjunction with a HYZ-3002-A ultra-weak light detector (Xi'an HeYongZhong Electronic Technology Co., Ltd.).

First, N-CQDs were synthesised via a hydro-thermal method(H. Y. Chen, Yuan, Jiang, Wang, & Zeng, 2022). To 20 mL of de-ionized water, were added 10 mmol of ammonium citrate and 670 μL of ethylenediamine. After stirring for 30 min, the resulting solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, followed by heating at 200 °C for 5 h. Upon completion of the reaction and cooling to room temperature, the resulting sample was dialysed (MWCO 1000) for 24 h, followed by centrifugation. These N-CQDs were then oven dried at 60 °C for 24 h.

N-CQDs@MIL-53 composites were prepared with slight modifications in literature procedures (X. Q. Chen, Liu, Zhu, Tao, & Wang, 2022; Yu et al., 2019). First, 1.3515 g of FeCl3·6H2O, 0.8306 g of H2BDC and 30 mL of DMF were mixed, followed by stirring for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous, transparent solution. A specified amount of the N-CQDs obtained were added to the mixture solution, followed by stirring for an additional hour. The reaction mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 3 h. After cooling, the solid product was separated via centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 3 min. The resulting solid was sequentially washed with N,N-dimethylformamide, ethanol and de-ionized water to remove any impurities. For comparison, MIL-53 was synthesised using the same method, without the addition of N-CQDs.

A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was employed as the working electrode, with an Ag/AgCl reference electrode and a platinum counter electrode. To ensure stable current flow, the surface area of the platinum wire counter electrode needs to be larger than that of the working electrode. The scanning potential was set in the range of 0 ∼ −1.9 V, with a scan rate of 0.2 V/s, and the photomultiplier voltage was adjusted to 800 V.

The GCE was sequentially polished using chamois leather and Al2O3 powder with particle sizes of 0.3 and 0.05 μm, respectively. After each polishing step, the GCE surface was thoroughly using de-ionized water to remove residual powder. The electrode was then ultra-sonically cleaned first in ethanol and then in de-ionized water. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was conducted in a 1.0 mmol/L K3Fe(CN)6 solution containing 0.1 mol/L KCl at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The pre-treated GCE was deemed ready for use when the potential difference between the oxidation and reduction peaks in the cyclic voltammogram was <80 mV.

Experiments were performed in 5 mL of a phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution with potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) used as a co-reactant. A 0.2 mg/mL chitosan solution was prepared by dissolving chitosan in a 1 % acetic acid solution. Thereafter, 1 mg of N-CQDs@MIL-53 was dispersed in 10 mL of de-ionized water to prepare a 0.1 mg/mL N-CQDs@MIL-53 composite solution. Both solutions were ultra-sonicated for 10 min before use to ensure uniform dispersion. For electrode modification, 3.0 μL of an aliquot from a 0.2 mg/mL chitosan solution was dropped onto the GCE surface. After allowing for the chitosan layer to slightly dry, 6.0 μL of a 0.1 mg/mL N-CQDs@MIL-53 composite solution was subsequently added. The film was then allowed to naturally dry at room temperature, resulting in a homogeneous layer suitable for ECL measurements. The schematic for the fabrication of the ECL sensor is shown in Scheme 1.

ECL efficiency was determined via the ratio of the number of emitted photons to that of electrons transferred. In a system containing 0.1 mol/L PBS and 0.1 mol/L K2S2O8, the relative ECL efficiency (
ϕ x
) of N-CQDs@MIL-53 can be calculated as follows.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the N-CQDs@MIL-53-based ECL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL sensor developed was used to measure the concentrations of Cu2+ in tap water, lake water, and two different brands of infant formulas.

The tap and lake water samples were collected from the Jilin University campus, and the infant formulas were sourced from a local supermarket in Changchun, China. For Cu2+ analysis in water samples, both the tap water and lake water were filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane, with no further pre-treatment required other than filtration.

To detect Cu2+ in infant formulas, 3.0 g of the powder was calcined in a crucible until it was completely converted into ash. The ash was then re-tempered in a muffle furnace at 600 °C for 2 h to ensure complete calcination. After cooling, the residue was dissolved in concentrated nitric acid, and the resulting solution was diluted using de-ionized water to adjust the pH to 6. Cu2+ concentrations in all samples were measured using the sensor developed. Each analysis was repeated thrice to ensure reproducibility, following which average Cu2+ concentrations were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of N-CQDs@MIL-53, N-CQDs and MIL-53
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of MIL-53 and N-CQDs@MIL-53 was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 1A and C illustrate the typical, hexagonal spindle-like shape of MIL-53, characterised by a sharp tip with lengths ranging from 5 to 6 μm and widths between 2 and 3 μm, consistent with the morphology observed in previous reports on MIL-53 synthesised under similar conditions(Chen, Liu, et al., 2022; T. Feng et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2021). By contrast, Fig. 1B and D show the morphology of N-CQDs@MIL-53, which is smaller in size, measuring ∼1–2 μm in length and 0.5 μm in width, with a similarly sharp tip. Notably, N-CQDs@MIL-53 exhibited a smoother surface texture than pristine MIL-53. This morphological difference suggested that N-CQDs incorporation altered the interaction between organic ligands and metal ions, influencing nucleation and growth during synthesis (C. Du et al., 2024).

We investigated the functional groups present in MIL-53, N-CQDs and N-CQDs@MIL-53 using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1E. For MIL-53, the peak at 550 cm−1, corresponding to the FeO bond, confirmed the presence of metal–oxygen interactions in MIL-53(Algarra et al., 2020; Han et al., 2018). Distinct peaks observed at 750 cm−1 and 1690 cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibration of the CH bond and the extension-contraction vibration of the CO bond within the benzene ring structure. Peaks at 1390 and 1540 cm−1 were ascribed to the symmetric and asymmetric vibrational modes of CO groups, respectively, indicating the successful synthesis of MIL-53(Chen, Yuan, et al., 2022). For N-CQDs, characteristic peaks at 1402 and 1576 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric vibrations of the CO and CO bonds. In addition, the peaks at 1657, 2923, 3261 and 3417 cm−1 suggested the presence of the CO, CH, –OH, and –NH2 groups, respectively, which were indicative of the amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CQDs(Yu et al., 2019). These polar groups enhanced the solubility and stability of N-CQDs@MIL-53 and may also have contributed to improved electron transfer(Seng, Tan, Lee, Ong, & Chai, 2020). The FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs@MIL-53 closely resembled those of MIL-53 and N-CQDs, with minor peak shifts observed at 1578 and 1700 cm−1. The absorption band at 480 cm−1 further supported the incorporation of N-CQDs, corresponding to their characteristic stretching vibration(Chen, Liu, et al., 2022).

The structures of the synthesised MIL-53 and N-CQDs@MIL-53 were characterised using X-ray diffraction (XRD), as shown in Fig. 1F. The XRD pattern of MIL-53 matches well with those in previously reported structures(Yu et al., 2019; Y. Zhang, Zhou, Chen, Feng, & Cai, 2020). MIL-53 exhibited characteristic diffraction peaks at 9.3°, 12.5°, 18.7°, 21.8° and 28.3°, wherein the peaks at 9.3° and 18.7° corresponded to the (100) and (002) crystal planes, respectively. The XRD diffraction peaks of N-CQDs@MIL-53 were essentially the same as those of MIL-53, indicating that the addition of N-CQDs during the hydro-thermal process had not altered the crystal phase of MIL-53. Meanwhile, the intensity of some peaks in N-CQDs@MIL-53 was stronger than that in MIL-53, suggesting that N-CQDs@MIL-53 possessed a higher degree of crystallinity. Owing to the very small particle size and amorphous nature of N-CQDs, no characteristic peaks associated with them were observed in the XRD pattern.

The surface chemical states of MIL-53 and N-CQDs@MIL-53 were investigated via X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS), as illustrated in Fig. 2 . The surface of MIL-53 primarily comprised the elements C, O and Fe, whereas N-CQDs/MIL-53 contained C, O, N and Fe (Fig. 2A). The C 1 s spectra (Fig. 2B) for both MIL-53 and N-CQDs@MIL-53 revealed two splitting peaks at 284.81 and 288.86 eV, which corresponded to the benzene ring and carboxyl functional groups of the H2BDC ligand, respectively(W. Mei et al., 2018; J. X. Xie, Tang, Chen, & Hao, 2023). The binding energy at 286.1 eV for N-CQDs@MIL-53 was attributed to the CN bond(Y. Yang et al., 2019). Upon comparing the O 1 s spectra of MIL-53 and N-CQDs@MIL-53 (Fig. 2C), two peaks at 532.03 and 531.32 eV were identified, corresponding to the FeO and CO bonds, respectively(Ma et al., 2021). For MIL-53, the primary characteristic peaks of the Fe 2p spectrum were observed at 711.9 and 725.3 eV (Fig. 2D), attributed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively(Kachhadiya & Murthy, 2023; Rezvani, Nabavi, & Ghani, 2023). The splitting energy (∆ = 2p1/2 − 2p3/2) of the 2p doublet was ∼13.4 eV, consistent with previous studies(Abuzalat, Tantawy, Mokhtar, & Baraka, 2022; Lv et al., 2015). Notably, the Fe 2p peak of N-CQDs@MIL-53 shifted to a higher binding energy, suggesting an increased oxidation state of Fe(W. D. Mei et al., 2019). This observation suggested that electrons had been drawn closer to the nucleus, which had reduced the electron density around Fe3+, thereby supporting the presence of an interaction between N-CQDs and MIL-53(Chen, Yuan, et al., 2022). These characterisation results clearly indicated that N-CQDs had been successfully chemically embedded in MIL-53 rather than being merely physically adsorbed(C. Du et al., 2024).

Fig. 3A shows the spooling ECL spectra of N-CQDs@MIL-53 in 0.1 mol/L K2S2O8. To obtain more data for detailed spectral analysis, spectral data were acquired at a scan rate of 0.1 V/s, spanning an applied potential range from 0 to −1.9 V. The data were analysed in three distinct segments based on different potential ranges: part 1 (forward scan, from −1.1 to −1.4 V; Fig. 3A(a)), part 2 (from −1.5 to −1.9 V, followed by a return to −1.7 V; Fig. 3A(b)) and part 3 (reverse scan, from −1.6 to −1.1 V; Fig. 3A(c)). Notably, ECL emission wavelengths exhibited significant shifts during the potential scan. ECL emission onset occurred at approximately −1.1 V, accompanied with a near-infrared (NIR) emission peak (Fig. 3A(a)). At this potential, N-CQDs@MIL-53 underwent reduction to form the corresponding anion (N-CQDs@MIL-53•−) (−1.1 V, Fig. S1C). As the scan proceeded to more negative potentials, the emission wavelength shifted to shorter wavelengths. For the potential ranging from −1.5 to −1.9 V and back to −1.7 V (Fig. 3A(b)), the ECL emission intensity peaked, with the emission in the visible region (680 to 760 nm) reaching its maximum. Upon scanning back from −1.6 to −1.1 V, the maximum emission wavelength of the spectra returned to the NIR region, and the ECL intensity gradually decreased, likely because of the consumption of SO4 •− and N-CQDs@MIL-53, as well as the reverse scan of the potential (Fig. 3A(c)). The trends observed in the spooling ECL spectra were consistent with that of the ECL-voltage curve shown in Fig. S1C, confirming the correlation between ECL evolution and degradation.

The UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption and FL emission spectra of N-CQDs@MIL-53 were investigated, as shown in Fig. 3B. N-CQDs exhibited an absorption peak at 337 nm, while MIL-53 showed a sharp absorption at 240 nm, which was attributed to charge transfer from the ligands to the metal, suggesting FeO bonding between carboxylate groups and metal centres(Panda, Rahut, & Basu, 2016; C. H. Zhang, Ai, & Jiang, 2015). Notably, the absorption spectrum of N-CQDs@MIL-53 did not exhibit a distinct peak associated with N-CQDs likely because of the low content of N-CQDs, which did not significantly affect the characteristic absorption of MIL-53(Yi & Zhang, 2020). FL emission peaks for N-CQDs and MIL-53 were observed at 448 and 472 nm, respectively, with excitation wavelengths of 320 and 280 nm, respectively. Upon excitation at 280 nm corresponding to MIL-53, N-CQDs@MIL-53 exhibited an FL emission peak at 469 nm, confirming the presence of MIL-53. Meanwhile, upon excitation at 320 nm corresponding to N-CQDs, N-CQDs@MIL-53 showed an FL emission peak at 432 nm. The observed blue shift relative to N-CQDs may be attributed to structural changes in N-CQDs during the synthesis process(D. X. Du et al., 2020). These results collectively supported the successful incorporation of N-CQDs into N-CQDs@MIL-53.

As shown in Fig. 3C, the maximum ECL emission wavelength of N-CQDs@MIL-53 (∼750 nm) exhibited a redshift relative to wavelengths associated with its FL emission (∼432 and ∼ 472 nm). In addition, the ECL emission wavelength underwent dynamic shifts during the potential scan. This phenomenon could primarily be attributed to the distinct mechanisms underlying the surface-state emission of ECL and the intrinsic-state emission of FL(Guo et al., 2022). In particularly, the ECL emission process involved multi-step electro-chemical reactions that required time to generate the excited state, which subsequently relaxed to the surface excited state. However, FL excitation was considerably faster. Generally, FL emission mainly originates from the core region, whereas ECL is highly sensitive to surface states(L. Q. Yang et al., 2021). Moreover, the observed shifts of ECL spectra during the potential scan are attributed to various functional groups containing C, N and O (–OH, –COOH, and –C=O) on the surface of N-CQDs, which generated multiple excited states with different energy levels, resulting in emissions at varying wavelengths(Qin et al., 2023). This was consistent with the ECL spooling data (Figs. 3A(a)–3A(c)), where, at the same scanning voltage, the excited-state species generated in the system were identical, resulting in an unchanged peak position in the ECL spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CV and EIS studies of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of the ECL sensor was characterised using CV and electro-chemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), using [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as the redox probe. As shown in Fig. 4A, after the application of CS, the electron transfer resistance (Rct) increased to become more than that of bare GCE. This suggested that CS had hindered the electron transfer process of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe. However, the addition of the N-CQDs@MIL-53 coating led to a significant reduction in Rct likely because of the increased effective electrode surface area, superior electrode area and remarkable electrical conductivity of N-CQDs@MIL-53.

The ECL sensor's performance was further characterised by CV current response. As shown in Fig. 4B, the application of CS decreased the peak current to be less than that of bare GCE, which could be attributed to the insulating nature of CS. As anticipated, the peak current increased after modifying the GCE via N-CQDs@MIL-53, indicating enhanced electron transfer. These CV results were consistent with the EIS data, further confirming that the electrical performance of the sensor improved upon incorporation of N-CQDs@MIL-53.

The annihilation pathway of the ECL process was initially investigated to investigate the ECL mechanism. As shown in Fig. S1A, the CV curve of the bare electrode in 0.1 mol/L PBS (pH 6) revealed no significant oxidation or reduction peaks, indicating a negligible background current. However, upon modification using N-CQDs@MIL-53, a distinct reduction peak appeared at −1.65 V, consistent with the electro-chemical reduction of N-CQDs@MIL-53 and the formation of anion radicals (N-CQDs@MIL-53•−). Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) analysis (Fig. S1B) further corroborated this observation by displaying two well-defined reduction peaks after background current correction. These reduction peaks closely aligned with those observed in the CV scan. However, no oxidation peaks were detected in either CV or DPV curves, and there was not any ECL signal observed in the ECL-potential profile of N-CQDs@MIL-53. This indicated that the anionic and cationic species generated by N-CQDs@MIL-53 likely had extremely short or unstable lifetimes, preventing the generation of ECL(Chu et al., 2020). Thus, although N-CQDs@MIL-53 underwent electro-chemical reduction to form anionic radicals, their instability severely restricted ECL emission. Aiming at addressing this restriction, K2S2O8 was selected as a co-reactant to explore the ECL emission behaviour of N-CQDs@MIL-53.

Because of observing the reduction peak during the annihilation process, K2S2O8 was introduced into the system as a co-reactant to investigate the ECL behaviour. As shown in Fig. S1C, ECL was barely observed in the absence of K2S2O8. However, a clear ECL signal generated upon the addition of K2S2O8. Moreover, the ECL signal from bare GCE was very weak in the presence of 0.1 mol/L K2S2O8, likely because of the limited electrical response from the reduction of S2O8 2−. ECL signals were observed to be notably enhanced after modifying the electrode surface using MIL-53 and N-CQDs@MIL-53, reaching 6750 and 16,592 a.u., respectively. This enhancement was attributed to the large surface area and porosity of MIL-53, which facilitated electron transfer and enhanced the ECL signal to be better than that of the bare electrode(Shan et al., 2023). Moreover, the N-CQDs@MIL-53-modified electrode exhibited a stable increase in ECL intensity, benefitting from the synergistic effects between N-CQDs and MIL-53 (Fig. 5C). The relative ECL efficiency of N-CQDs@MIL-53 was calculated to be 0.17 (vs. Ru(bpy)3 2+/K2S2O8).

Fig. S1E shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cu2+ and the ECL emission spectrum of N-CQDs@MIL-53. The minimal overlap between the two spectral curves confirmed that Cu2+ did not quench the ECL signal of N-CQDs@MIL-53 via a resonance energy transfer mechanism. Further investigation revealed that the introduction of Cu2+ into N-CQDs@MIL-53 generated a new absorption band at ∼230 nm. This band was not observed in the spectrum of Cu2+ alone (grey curve). Similarly, as shown in Fig. S1F, a comparable absorption band (green curve) was observed upon adding Cu2+ into an N-CQDs solution. Infrared spectroscopy analysis confirmed that N-CQDs possessed surface amino functional groups. This suggested that the new absorption band originated from interactions between Cu2+ and surface amino groups of N-CQDs, leading to the formation of copper-amine complexes.

In summary, the ECL mechanism was deduced as follows: Initially, an electro-chemical reduction process of N-CQDs@MIL-53 occurs on the GCE surface, leading to the generation of N-CQDs@MIL-53•−. Simultaneously, S2O8 2− reduces into SO4 •− and SO4 2−. The N-CQDs@MIL-53•− generated then interacts with the highly reactive SO4 •−, leading to the formation of the excited-state N-CQDs@MIL-53⁎. As N-CQDs@MIL-53⁎ relaxes to its ground state, energy is released in the form of ECL emission.

The concentration of N-CQDs@MIL-53, pH and the concentration of K2S2O8 were optimised to explore the optimal performance of the ECL sensor.

First, the ratios of N-CQDs to H2BDC were set at 1:50, 1:40, 1:20, 1:10, and 1:5 to synthesise five different N-CQDs@MIL-53 composite materials. Subsequently, the ECL signal intensities of these five composites, each at the same concentration, were measured under identical experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. S2A, the ECL intensity increased with the doping amount of N-CQDs. However, the ECL intensity began to decline when the ratio exceeded 1:20. This suggested that (1) there is an optimal ratio for achieving the optimal combined effect of N-CQDs and MIL-53 and (2) an excess of N-CQDs may have led to absorption or scattering of the ECL emission(D. F. Feng et al., 2019). In light of these results, a N-CQDs to H2BDC ratio of 1:10 was selected for the preparation of the N-CQDs@MIL-53 composite material.

When 6 μL of N-CQDs@MIL-53 was modified onto the electrode surface, an increase in ECL intensity was observed as the N-CQD@MIL-53 concentration was increased from 0.05 to 0.10 mg/mL (Fig. S2B). However, the ECL intensity began to decrease at concentrations exceeding 0.10 mg/mL. This decline is attributed to the saturation of the electrode surface with a fixed volume of N-CQDs@MIL-53. Once the concentration exceeded a certain threshold, the N-CQDs@MIL-53 layer on the electrode became too thick, impeding efficient electron transfer(Shan et al., 2023). Therefore, an N-CQDs@MIL-53 concentration of 0.1 mg/mL was determined to be optimal.

In addition, we investigated the effect of buffer solution pH, within the range of 5–9, on the performance of the ECL sensor (Fig. S2C). The results indicated a slight increase in ECL intensity at pHs 5 and 6, but the overall change was minimal. However, the ECL intensity decreased with continuous rise in the pH. This decrease could be attributed to two factors: (1) at low pH, protons were more easily reduced at negative potentials, hindering the reduction of N-CQDs@MIL-53 and (2) at high pH, OH− consumed SO4 •− generated from the reduction of K2S2O8, reducing ECL signal intensity(X. Y. Liu et al., 2025). Therefore, the optimal pH of the buffer solution was determined to be 6.

As shown in Fig. S2D, an insufficient reaction between K2S2O8 and N-CQDs@MIL-53 at low K2S2O8 concentrations resulted in weak ECL intensity. The ECL intensity approached its maximum when the K2S2O8 concentration was increased to 0.10 mol/L, and further increases in concentration did not significantly enhance the ECL signal. This suggested that 0.10 mol/L K2S2O8 was sufficient to maintain a stable ECL output in the system. Thus, 0.10 mol/L was selected as the optimal concentration for K2S2O8. These systematic optimisations resulted in the identification of the most efficient operational parameters for the sensor, improving its sensitivity, stability and reliability, thus enhancing its potential for practical analysis and detection.

Furthermore, we investigated the effect of dissolved oxygen on the ECL signal of N-CQDs@MIL-53. At 0.1 mol/L K2S2O8, ECL measurements of N-CQDs@MIL-53 were conducted in both de‑oxygenated PBS and non-de‑oxygenated PBS solutions. Fig. S3 shows that the ECL intensity of N-CQDs@MIL-53 in de‑oxygenated PBS solution was approximately the same as that in the non-de‑oxygenated PBS solution, thereby eliminating the influence of the possibility of dissolved oxygen on the ECL reaction of the system.

Under optimised conditions, the ECL sensor was employed to detect Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 5A, the ECL intensity decreased as the Cu2+ concentration was increased, in the range from 5 × 10−8 to 1 × 10−4 mol/L. A linear relationship was observed between the logarithm of the Cu2+ concentration and the degree of ECL quenching (Fig. 5B): %(I 0 – I)/I 0 = 19.46 × lgC – 28.17 (R 2 = 0.9974). The limit of detection (LOD) was observed to be 1.44 × 10−8 mol/L (0.92 ppb), and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) was 4.80 × 10−8 mol/L (3.05 ppb). These values were well less than the World Health Organization's permissible limit for Cu2+ in drinking water, which is 2 ppm.

To compare the performance of the N-CQDs@MIL-53-based ECL sensor, the ECL produced on a GCE modified with 0.05 mg/mL N-CQDs was investigated, with all other experimental conditions identical to those used for the N-CQDs@MIL-53-based sensor. A decrease in ECL intensity was observed as the Cu2+ concentration was increased within the range from 5 × 10−6 to 6 × 10−5 mol/L. In this concentration range, a linear correlation was observed between the logarithm of the Cu2+ concentration and the degree of ECL quenching (Fig. 5B): %(I 0 – I)/I 0 = 13.38 × lgC – 32.98, (R 2 = 0.9993). The LOD was determined to be 1.22 × 10−6 mol/L and the LOQ to be 4.06 × 10−6 mol/L.

The comparison of the two linear curves demonstrated that integrating N-CQDs with MIL-53 not only expanded the detection range but also resulted in enhanced sensitivity. This improvement could be attributed to the porous structure of MIL-53, which increased the available surface area for reactions and facilitated the efficient transport. Furthermore, the synergistic interaction between MIL-53 and N-CQDs potentially generated a greater number of reactive sites, further enhancing the sensor's performance. In comparison with previously reported Cu2+ detection methods (Table S1), the ECL sensor developed offered a Cu2+ detection limit that was comparable or even better, while also extending the detection range to meet requirements for effective analysis.

The performance of the ECL sensor prepared was evaluated in terms of stability, selectivity and reproducibility. The stability of the sensor was assessed through continuous cyclic scanning for 700 s (37 cycles) on a GCE modified with 0.10 mg/mL N-CQDs@MIL-53 (6 μL). As shown in Fig. 5C, the ECL intensity exhibited minimal variation, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of only 1.19 %, indicating remarkable stability. For comparison, the stability of ECL generated on a GCE modified with N-CQDs alone was also tested (Fig. S4), further demonstrating the superior stability of the N-CQDs@MIL-53-based sensor. These results highlighted the robustness of the sensor fabricated herein and its potential for reliable analytical applications.

To evaluate the selectivity of the ECL sensor fabricated, its ECL responses to various metal ions (Fe2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+ and Cu2+) at an identical concentration (2 × 10−5 mol/L) were measured under comparable experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 5D, the presence of these interfering ions caused negligible quenching of the ECL signal. However, Cu2+ induced a quenching effect approximately five to six times stronger than that caused by the other ions, highlighting the sensor's remarkable selectivity for Cu2+.

To assess the stability of the electrodes, their ECL intensity was periodically measured under optimised conditions (S/N = 3). As shown in Fig. 5E, the ECL intensity generated on the electrode modified by N-CQDs decreased by 52.88 % across a five-day period. However, the N-CQDs@MIL-53 modified electrode retained >96.15 % of its initial signal, exhibiting significantly improved stability. This enhancement could be attributed to the role of MOFs as remarkable protective matrices for encapsulating functional materials. The incorporation of N-CQDs within MOFs ensured uniform dispersion(Z. Wang et al., 2024), while the spatial confinement of N-CQDs within MOF cavities prevented their aggregation(Balakrishnan, Ang, & Mahmoudi, 2023), effectively mitigating degradation and further enhancing the stability of N-CQDs@MIL-53.

The reproducibility of the N-CQDs@MIL-53-based ECL sensor was assessed by measuring the ECL signal intensities of five identically prepared electrodes under identical experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 5F, the results indicated remarkable reproducibility, with the RSD of the ECL intensity measured to be only 1.82 %. This high reproducibility highlighted the reliability of the sensor fabrication process and the stability of the N-CQDs@MIL-53-modified electrode.

Cu2+ concentrations in tap water, lake water and infant formula samples were measured to evaluate the practical application of the ECL sensor. Cu2+ concentrations in these samples were also quantified using inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES). ICP-OES results revealed no detectable Cu2+ in the tap water and lake water samples, and the Cu2+ concentrations in infant formula sample 1 and infant formula sample 2 were observed to be 3.52 and 3.39 mg/kg, respectively.

The ECL sensor fabricated was then employed to detect Cu2+ in these samples. Similarly, no Cu2+ was detected in the water samples. Therefore, a spiking recovery experiment was conducted by adding known amounts of Cu2+ into the water samples. The recovery rates ranged from 95.8 % to 107.4 %, with RSDs being between 1.0 % and 6.9 %. For infant formula samples 1 and 2, the ECL sensor detected Cu2+ concentrations to be 3.76 mg/kg and 3.51 mg/kg, respectively, with relative errors of 6.88 % and 3.67 % in comparison with the ICP-OES results, respectively.

The results, summarised in Tables 1 and 2
, indicated that the ECL sensor held considerable potential for accurately measuring Cu2+ concentrations both in water and infant formula samples.

N-CQDs were successfully embedded into MIL-53 via a hydro-thermal method to form an N-CQDs@MIL-53 composite. This composite was employed to fabricate an ECL sensor for the quantification of Cu2+. The porous structure of MIL-53 facilitated the enrichment of SO4 •−, thereby reducing the electron transfer distance and improving the luminous efficiency of N-CQDs@MIL-53. Moreover, the integration of N-CQDs into MIL-53 ensured their uniform distribution and minimised aggregation, which significantly enhanced the storage stability of N-CQDs. Furthermore, compared with the ECL sensor fabricated using N-CQDs, the N-CQDs@MIL-53-based sensor showed a notable improvement both in sensitivity and detection range. The sensor fabricated exhibited a low LOD (1.44 × 10−8 mol/L) and a wide linear response range (from 5 × 10−8 to 1 × 10−4 mol/L), offering remarkable sensitivity and linearity. When employed for the detection of Cu2+ in water and infant formula samples, the sensor's results were consistent with those obtained using ICP-OES, demonstrating its effectiveness. Overall, this study provided a simple, cost-efficient method for Cu2+ analysis in actual samples and introduced a promising strategy to enhance the sensitivity and stability of CQDs-based detection methods.

Xiaofeng Wei: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yu Su: Methodology, Investigation. Man Jiang: Methodology, Investigation. Hongfei Ji: Methodology, Investigation. Ruizhong Zhang: Methodology. Daqian Song: Conceptualization. Lan Ding: Writing – review & editing. Yanhua Chen: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
